sections.heading: Blind Search
sections.body: Uninformed (or "blind") search strategies have no extra information about how close a state is to the goal beyond what's given in the problem definition itself — they can only distinguish a goal state from a non-goal state, with no sense of "getting warmer."

sections.heading: Tree Search
sections.body: A tree-search version of an algorithm expands nodes without checking whether a state has already been visited. This is simple to implement but can waste time re-exploring the same state repeatedly if the state space has multiple paths to the same state.

sections.heading: Graph Search
sections.body: A graph-search version keeps track of states already visited (an "explored set") and skips re-expanding them. This avoids redundant work but requires extra memory to store the explored set.

sections.heading: Completeness
sections.body: An uninformed search strategy is complete if it's guaranteed to find a solution whenever one exists — for example, BFS is complete on finite state spaces, while a naive DFS can fail to terminate on an infinite or very deep state space.

sections.heading: Optimality
sections.body: An uninformed search strategy is optimal if it always finds the cheapest solution, not just any solution. Whether a given strategy is optimal often depends on whether all action costs are equal or vary — this distinction is exactly why BFS, DFS, and UCS behave differently, covered in the following topics.

quiz.question: What defines an "uninformed" or "blind" search strategy?
options: It has no extra information about distance to the goal beyond the problem definition
It always finds the optimal solution
It only works on graphs, never trees

quiz.question: What does graph search add compared to tree search?
options: Tracking already-visited states to avoid redundant re-exploration

quiz.question: A search strategy is "complete" if it:
options: Is guaranteed to find a solution whenever one exists
Never explores more than one node
